content:
# Exodus Chapter 38 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), pp. 109–110; Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 81–82 | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/EXO_038_page_001.jpg`, `source-texts/hebrew-OT/EXO_038_page_002.jpg`, `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/EXO_038_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`, `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/EXO_038_vaticanus_page_002.jpg`

---

### The Bronze Altar (vv. 1–7)

**1** He made the altar for burnt offerings out of acacia wood — seven and a half feet square and four and a half feet tall. **2** He made horns at each of its four corners, forming them as one piece with the altar itself, and overlaid the whole thing with bronze. **3** He made all the altar's equipment — the pots for removing ashes, the shovels, the sprinkling basins, the meat forks, and the firepans — all of them out of bronze. **4** He made a grating for the altar, a mesh network of bronze, and placed it below the altar's ledge, reaching halfway up from the bottom. **5** He cast four rings at the four corners of the bronze grating to hold the carrying poles. **6** He made the poles of acacia wood and overlaid them with bronze, **7** then inserted the poles through the rings on either side of the altar so it could be carried. He made the altar hollow, out of planks.

---

### The Bronze Basin (v. 8)

**8** He made the bronze basin and its bronze stand from the mirrors donated by the women who served at the entrance to the tent of meeting.

---

### The Courtyard (vv. 9–20)

**9** Next he made the courtyard. On the south side, the courtyard hangings were of finely woven linen, stretching one hundred and fifty feet. **10** They hung from twenty posts set in twenty bronze bases, with silver hooks and connecting rods on the posts. **11** On the north side as well, the hangings stretched one hundred and fifty feet, with twenty posts and twenty bronze bases, and silver hooks and connecting rods on the posts. **12** On the west side, the hangings stretched seventy-five feet, with ten posts and ten bases, and silver hooks and connecting rods on the posts. **13** The east side, facing the sunrise, was also seventy-five feet across. **14** On one side of the entrance, the hangings extended twenty-two and a half feet, with three posts and three bases. **15** On the other side of the entrance — on both sides of the courtyard gate — the hangings also extended twenty-two and a half feet, with three posts and three bases. **16** All the hangings around the courtyard were of finely woven linen. **17** The bases for the posts were bronze, while the hooks and connecting rods on the posts were silver. The tops of the posts were overlaid with silver, and all the courtyard posts were banded with silver.

**18** The screen for the courtyard entrance was the work of a skilled embroiderer, made of blue, purple, and scarlet yarn and finely woven linen. It was thirty feet long and — matching the courtyard hangings — seven and a half feet tall. **19** It hung from four posts set in four bronze bases, with silver hooks, silver-overlaid tops, and silver connecting rods. **20** All the tent pegs for the tabernacle and for the surrounding courtyard were bronze.

---

### The Accounting of Materials (vv. 21–31)

**21** Here is the inventory of materials used for the tabernacle — the tabernacle of the covenant law — recorded at Moses' command. This accounting was the work of the Levites under the direction of Ithamar, son of Aaron the priest. **22** Bezalel son of Uri, son of Hur, from the tribe of Judah, made everything that YHWH had commanded Moses. **23** Working alongside him was Oholiab son of Ahisamach, from the tribe of Dan — an engraver, a designer, and an embroiderer in blue, purple, and scarlet yarn and fine linen.

**24** The total gold used in all the work on the sanctuary — the gold from the wave offering — came to twenty-nine talents and seven hundred and thirty shekels, measured by the sanctuary standard. **25** The silver collected from those in the community who were counted in the census came to one hundred talents and one thousand seven hundred and seventy-five shekels, measured by the sanctuary standard — **26** one beka per person (that is, half a shekel by the sanctuary standard) from everyone who was counted in the census, twenty years old and above: six hundred and three thousand, five hundred and fifty men. **27** The hundred talents of silver were used to cast the bases for the sanctuary and the bases for the curtain — one hundred bases from one hundred talents, one talent per base. **28** From the remaining one thousand seven hundred and seventy-five shekels he made the hooks for the posts, overlaid their tops, and made their connecting rods.

**29** The bronze from the wave offering totaled seventy talents and two thousand four hundred shekels. **30** With it he made the bases for the entrance to the tent of meeting, the bronze altar with its bronze grating and all the altar's equipment, **31** the bases for the surrounding courtyard, the bases for the courtyard entrance, and all the tent pegs for the tabernacle and for the surrounding courtyard.

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

### Verse 1 — The bronze altar
The מִזְבַּח הָעֹלָה (mizbeach ha'olah, "altar of the burnt offering") was the largest furnishing in the tabernacle complex — five cubits square and three cubits high (approximately 7.5 x 7.5 x 4.5 feet / 2.25 x 2.25 x 1.35 m). Unlike the gold-overlaid incense altar of the Holy Place (37:25–28), this altar was overlaid with bronze (נְחֹשֶׁת, nechoshet), appropriate for the intense heat of animal sacrifice. The word עֹלָה (olah, "burnt offering") comes from the root ע-ל-ה (to go up, to ascend) — the offering that "goes up" entirely as smoke to God, nothing held back. This altar stood in the courtyard between the entrance and the tabernacle itself, making it the first sacred object any worshiper encountered. The construction account here follows the instructions given in 27:1–8. The LXX renders θυσιαστήριον τῶν ὁλοκαυτωμάτων (altar of whole burnt offerings), using the term ὁλοκαύτωμα that emphasizes the offering being "wholly burned."

### Verse 8 — The women's mirrors
This verse is one of the most striking in the entire construction narrative. The כִּיּוֹר (kiyyor, "basin" or "laver") for priestly washing was made from the מַרְאֹת (mar'ot, "mirrors") of the הַצֹּבְאֹת (hatsov'ot, "the serving/assembling women") who gathered אֲשֶׁר צָבְאוּ פֶּתַח אֹהֶל מוֹעֵד (asher tsav'u petach ohel mo'ed, "who served at the entrance of the tent of meeting"). The bronze mirrors — polished metal surfaces used for seeing one's reflection — were transformed into a vessel for purification. The women who donated them are described with the verb צָבָא (tsava), the same word used for military service or organized duty, suggesting these women had a recognized role of service at the sanctuary. The LXX renders τῶν νηστευσασῶν (ton nesteusason, "of the fasting women"), a significantly different interpretation — reading the women as ascetics rather than organized servants. The Vaticanus text's reading points to a different understanding of who these women were, but both traditions agree on the remarkable nature of the gift: instruments of personal vanity reshaped into an instrument of sacred cleansing. The basin is where Aaron and his sons would wash their hands and feet before approaching the altar or entering the tent (30:18–21), on pain of death. That it was made from women's mirrors — an intensely personal possession in the ancient world — speaks to the depth of communal sacrifice involved in building the tabernacle.

### Verses 9–20 — The courtyard dimensions
The חָצֵר (chatser, "courtyard") measured 100 cubits (approximately 150 feet / 45 m) on the north and south sides, and 50 cubits (approximately 75 feet / 23 m) on the east and west sides, creating a rectangular enclosure around the tabernacle. The hangings were קְלָעִים (qela'im, "curtains/hangings") of שֵׁשׁ מָשְׁזָר (shesh moshzar, "twisted/woven fine linen") — the same high-quality linen used throughout the tabernacle. The courtyard wall stood five cubits (7.5 feet / 2.25 m) tall, shielding the interior from casual view. The Hebrew carefully details every post (עַמּוּד, ammud), base (אֶדֶן, eden), hook (וָו, vav), and connecting rod (חֲשׁוּקִים, chashuqim), emphasizing the material distinction: bases of bronze (grounding), hooks, rods, and post-caps of silver (connecting and crowning). The entrance screen on the east side — the direction of sunrise, the direction one faced the sanctuary — was the work of a רֹקֵם (roqem, "embroiderer") in the characteristic blue (תְּכֵלֶת, tekhelet), purple (אַרְגָּמָן, argaman), and scarlet (תּוֹלַעַת שָׁנִי, tola'at shani) that marked every transition point in the sacred space. The dynamic rendering "skilled embroiderer" captures the specialized artisan expertise implied by the Hebrew term.

### Verses 21–23 — The accounting and its administrators
The word פְּקוּדֵי (pequdei, "accountings/reckonings") that opens the section — and gives this portion of Exodus its traditional Hebrew name (Parashat Pekudei) — comes from the root פ-ק-ד (p-q-d, "to attend to, to number, to muster"). This is not merely bookkeeping but a solemn mustering, an official reckoning. The accounting was performed עַל־פִּי מֹשֶׁה (al-pi Mosheh, "by the mouth/command of Moses") as the work of the Levites בְּיַד אִיתָמָר (beyad Ithamar, "under the hand of Ithamar"), Aaron's youngest surviving son. The phrase עֲבֹדַת הַלְוִיִּם (avodat haleviyyim, "the service of the Levites") uses עֲבֹדָה (avodah), which means both "work/labor" and "worship/service" — the accounting itself was an act of sacred duty. The naming of Bezalel (בְּצַלְאֵל, "in the shadow of God") and Oholiab (אָהֳלִיאָב, "the father's tent") together reaffirms the partnership between Judah and Dan — the most prominent and one of the smallest tribes — in the construction. The LXX preserves these names (Βεσελεηλ and Ελιαβ) and the administrative structure. The dynamic rendering "covenant law" for עֵדוּת (edut, "testimony") reflects that this word refers to the covenant stipulations housed in the ark.

### Verses 24–28 — The gold and silver accounting
The quantities are staggering. The gold totaled twenty-nine talents and 730 shekels — a talent (כִּכָּר, kikkar) was approximately 75 pounds (34 kg), making the total roughly 2,195 pounds (996 kg) of pure gold. The silver came to exactly 100 talents and 1,775 shekels. The silver was not a freewill offering but a mandatory census tax: one בֶּקַע (beqa, "half") — that is, half a shekel — from every man twenty years old and above. The census count given is 603,550, which matches Numbers 1:46 precisely. The math is transparent: 603,550 half-shekels = 301,775 shekels; at 3,000 shekels per talent, that yields 100 talents and 1,775 shekels — exactly the figure reported. This precision serves a theological purpose: it demonstrates that every single Israelite man contributed equally to the foundation of the sanctuary. The 100 talents of silver became the 100 אֲדָנִים (adanim, "bases/sockets") — the foundation upon which the entire tabernacle rested. Every base represented one talent, and every talent represented approximately 6,035 men's contributions. The sanctuary literally stood on the equal participation of all the people. The remaining 1,775 shekels were used for the hooks, post-caps, and connecting rods — the elements that held the structure together. The שֶׁקֶל הַקֹּדֶשׁ (sheqel haqqodesh, "sanctuary shekel") was the standardized weight used for all sacred transactions, likely heavier than the common commercial shekel, ensuring precision in sacred matters.

### Verses 29–31 — The bronze accounting
The bronze totaled 70 talents and 2,400 shekels — approximately 5,295 pounds (2,402 kg). Bronze was used for the most public and exposed elements: the bases at the tent entrance, the great altar of burnt offering and its grating and utensils, the courtyard bases and entrance bases, and all the tent pegs (יְתֵדוֹת, yetedot) for both the tabernacle and the courtyard. A clear material hierarchy emerges across the accounting: gold for the innermost and most holy objects (ark, mercy seat, lampstand, incense altar), silver for the structural foundations and connectors, and bronze for the outer and utilitarian elements. This gradient of precious metals from interior to exterior mirrors the gradient of holiness from the Most Holy Place outward to the courtyard — the material world itself organized to express the theology of approaching the divine.

### Vaticanus Divergences
The LXX in Codex Vaticanus presents the material corresponding to MT Exodus 38 with significant structural differences, continuing the pattern observed throughout the construction narrative. (1) The Vaticanus text visible on pages 81–82 shows chapter heading ΚΕΦ. ΛVIII (Chapter 38) at the top, but the content arrangement differs substantially from the MT. The LXX includes in its chapter 38 material about the priestly garments that the MT places in chapter 39, while some of the courtyard and accounting material the MT places in chapter 38 appears in different locations in the Greek text. (2) The bronze altar construction in the LXX is more compressed, with less repetitive detail about the construction process. (3) The accounting section — one of the most distinctive passages in the MT — is present in the LXX but positioned differently and with some variations in the reported figures. The LXX census figure and talent calculations show minor textual variations in some manuscripts, though the Vaticanus text largely agrees with the MT's numbers. (4) The verse about the women's mirrors (MT 38:8) is preserved in the LXX but with the notable variant reading "fasting women" (νηστευσασῶν) rather than "serving women" — a difference that may reflect different vocalization traditions of the Hebrew or a different theological emphasis in the Greek translation tradition. (5) The courtyard description in the LXX is present but with the typical compression of repetitive elements. Overall, the MT's chapter 38 serves a unique literary function: it moves from construction (altar, basin, courtyard) to accounting (gold, silver, bronze), creating a transition from the narrative of making to the narrative of reckoning — a pause for inventory before the final assembly. This structural movement from craft to census, from building to counting, carries theological weight: before the tabernacle can be erected and filled with divine glory (ch. 40), every contribution must be acknowledged, every resource traced to its source, every person counted.
